title: Function Definition
description: The function's actual body: the header line, matching a declaration, and what happens when definition and declaration disagree.
objectives: Understand what a function definition is, as distinct from its declaration
Write a function definition with a matching header and a body
Know that a definition must match its declaration exactly
Understand a function can only be defined once (One Definition Rule, informally)

sections.text: A function definition provides the function's actual body — the real code that runs when the function is called.
Where a declaration (int add(int, int);) only promises the function exists, a definition delivers on that promise with real, runnable code.
2. The Two Halves of a Definition

rows: Return type, name, and parameters — same shape as a declaration

sections.text: A definition's header looks exactly like a declaration, minus the semicolon — because a definition includes a declaration inside it; it just adds the body right after.
3. The Header Must Match the Declaration
If a declaration says add returns int, the definition must also return int — the return type, name, and parameter types all need to agree. A mismatch is a compilation error, not a warning.

Unlike a declaration, which can technically appear multiple times (as long as they all agree), a function can only be defined once in a program. Defining add twice, even identically, is a compilation error — recall from main() Function that having two definitions of the same function causes exactly this kind of conflict.

A separate declaration is only necessary when the function is called before its definition appears in the file. If the full definition comes first, it already tells the compiler everything it needs — no separate prototype required.

sections.title: The chapter itself, not just its listing
sections.text: If a declaration is a table-of-contents entry promising "Chapter 5 exists," the definition is the actual Chapter 5 — the real content someone can read and follow. A book can only have one "real" Chapter 5; two different versions of the same chapter in one book would be a contradiction.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Writing a definition whose header doesn't match its declaration — return type, name, and parameter types must all agree.
Defining the same function twice in the same program — even identical definitions conflict; only one is allowed.
Forgetting the body entirely and leaving just a header with a semicolon — that's a declaration, not a definition.
Assuming a declaration is always required — it's only needed when the function is called before its definition appears.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a function definition?
questions.answer: The function's actual body — the real, runnable code that executes when the function is called, as opposed to just its declaration.

questions.question: Does a function definition's header need to match its declaration?
questions.answer: Yes — the return type, name, and parameter types must all agree between the declaration and the definition.

questions.question: Can a function be defined more than once in a program?
questions.answer: No — a function can only have one definition; defining it twice, even identically, is a compilation error.

questions.question: Is a separate declaration always required before a function definition?
questions.answer: No — a declaration is only needed if the function is called before its definition appears in the file; a definition placed before its first use already provides everything the compiler needs.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A function definition provides the actual body of a function — the real code that runs when it's called — unlike a declaration, which only announces that the function exists. A definition's header must exactly match any earlier declaration in return type, name, and parameters, and a function can only be defined once in a program. If a function is fully defined before it's first called, no separate declaration is needed; a declaration only matters when the call happens earlier in the file than the definition.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Function Definition
sections.text: Definition = the real chapter, not just its listing. Header must match the promise made earlier. Only one real chapter allowed per book.
